1â†’# ~/claude/CLAUDE.md
     2â†’
     3â†’claude's home folder, working directory, and headquarters. everything here belongs to claude â€” change freely.
     4â†’
     5â†’<skogai_todo>
     6â†’
     7â†’for anything task-related â€” planning, creating, executing, reviewing, closing â€” use the `task-manager` agent. it handles the full lifecycle via `skogai-todo` and `gh`.
     8â†’
     9â†’</skogai_todo>
    10â†’
    11â†’<skogapi>
    12â†’
    13â†’- skogapi: FastAPI service at localhost:9999 â€” wraps skogcli, skogparse, agents, config, scripts
    14â†’- service: `systemctl --user restart skogai-skogapi` â€” start script at `~/.config/systemd/skogai-skogapi-start.sh`
    15â†’- code: `projects/skogapi/main.py`
    16â†’- systemd services follow pattern: `skogai-*.service` + `~/.config/systemd/skogai-*-start.sh`
    17â†’- env needed in service: `SKOGAI_CONFIG_DIR=/skogai/config`, `PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"`
    18â†’- agents via `skogcli agent list/read/create`, NOT file directories
    19â†’- `uvx` isolates env â€” always use full paths or export PATH in start scripts
    20â†’
    21â†’</skogapi>
    22â†’
    23â†’## Project Structure
    24â†’
    25â†’- @.cass/ - CASS Memory playbook and blocked patterns (cm)
    26â†’- @.claude-plugin/ - Plugin configuration (plugin.json, marketplace.json)
    27â†’- @commands/ - Custom slash commands (plugin component)
    28â†’- @docs/claude-code/ - Extensive Claude Code documentation
    29â†’- @docs/skogix/ - User documentation (definitions.md, user.md)
    30â†’- @skills/ - Custom skills for Claude Code (plugin component)
    31â†’  - @skills/claude-code-modifications/SKILL.md - main project skill
    32â†’  - @skills/claude-code-modifications/scripts/update-plugins.sh - plugin update/reload
    33â†’- @lessons/ - Keyword-triggered behavioral lessons (gptme format, injected via hooks)
    34â†’- @JOINK/ - Reference implementation of lesson system from gptme
    35â†’- @.todo/ - Curated reference collection (hooks, skills, demodotfiles, templates)
    36â†’- @todo/ - Active review items (rtk-optimized.md)
    37â†’
    38â†’## Tools
    39â†’
    40â†’- NEVER use sed or awk -- use proper tools (yq, jq, etc.)
    41â†’- `yq --front-matter=extract '<query>' file.md` -- parse YAML frontmatter from markdown
    42â†’- when a tool version is wrong, ask skogix to install the correct one
    43â†’
    44â†’### RTK (Rust Token Killer)
    45â†’
    46â†’Token-optimized command wrapper. Use `rtk` prefix for high-verbosity commands:
    47â†’
    48â†’- `rtk git log` (92% reduction), `rtk git status` (76%), `rtk git diff` (56%), `rtk find` (76%)
    49â†’- Do NOT use for: `ls` (-274% worse), `grep` (buggy in v0.2.0)
    50â†’- Skip when using native Claude tools (Grep/Read/Glob) or for small outputs
    51â†’- Reference: @todo/rtk-optimized.md, @.todo/skills/rtk-optimizer/SKILL.md
    52â†’
    53â†’### ms (Meta Skill)
    54â†’
    55â†’Skill management CLI. 800+ skills indexed across the system.
    56â†’
    57â†’```bash
    58â†’ms suggest --explain         # context-aware skill recommendations with scoring
    59â†’ms search "query"            # hybrid BM25 + semantic search
    60â†’ms load <skill> --level full # load skill with progressive disclosure
    61â†’ms lint                      # validate skill files
    62â†’ms dedup scan                # find duplicate skills
    63â†’ms doctor                    # health check
    64â†’ms list                      # list indexed skills (local index ~54, full scan ~814)
    65â†’ms index --all /home/skogix  # re-index everything
    66â†’```
    67â†’
    68â†’- All commands support `-O json` for machine-readable output
    69â†’- MCP server available: `ms mcp serve` (stdio for Claude Code)
    70â†’- Config: `.ms/config.toml` per project, `~/.config/ms/config.toml` global
    71â†’- Reference: @SKILL.md (ms skill definition at repo root)
    72â†’
    73â†’## Lessons System
    74â†’
    75â†’- @lessons/ contains keyword-triggered behavioral lessons (gptme format)
    76â†’- lessons are NOT skills -- they are small contextual injections ("hey remember this")
    77â†’- @JOINK/ has the reference implementation (python lesson system from gptme)
    78â†’- don't touch lesson content, don't reorganize lessons -- build the injection mechanism
    79â†’
    80â†’<!-- br-agent-instructions-v1 -->
    81â†’
    82â†’---
    83â†’
    84â†’## Beads Workflow Integration
    85â†’
    86â†’This project uses [beads_rust](https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_rust) (`br`/`bd`) for issue tracking. Issues are stored in `.beads/` and tracked in git.
    87â†’
    88â†’### Essential Commands
    89â†’
    90â†’```bash
    91â†’# View ready issues (unblocked, not deferred)
    92â†’br ready              # or: bd ready
    93â†’
    94â†’# List and search
    95â†’br list --status=open # All open issues
    96â†’br show <id>          # Full issue details with dependencies
    97â†’br search "keyword"   # Full-text search
    98â†’
    99â†’# Create and update
   100â†’br create --title="..." --description="..." --type=task --priority=2
   101â†’br update <id> --status=in_progress
   102â†’br close <id> --reason="Completed"
   103â†’br close <id1> <id2>  # Close multiple issues at once
   104â†’
   105â†’# Sync with git
   106â†’br sync --flush-only  # Export DB to JSONL
   107â†’br sync --status      # Check sync status
   108â†’```
   109â†’
   110â†’### Workflow Pattern
   111â†’
   112â†’1. **Start**: Run `br ready` to find actionable work
   113â†’2. **Claim**: Use `br update <id> --status=in_progress`
   114â†’3. **Work**: Implement the task
   115â†’4. **Complete**: Use `br close <id>`
   116â†’5. **Sync**: Always run `br sync --flush-only` at session end
   117â†’
   118â†’### Key Concepts
   119â†’
   120â†’- **Dependencies**: Issues can block other issues. `br ready` shows only unblocked work.
   121â†’- **Priority**: P0=critical, P1=high, P2=medium, P3=low, P4=backlog (use numbers 0-4, not words)
   122â†’- **Types**: task, bug, feature, epic, chore, docs, question
   123â†’- **Blocking**: `br dep add <issue> <depends-on>` to add dependencies
   124â†’
   125â†’### Session Protocol
   126â†’
   127â†’**Before ending any session, run this checklist:**
   128â†’
   129â†’```bash
   130â†’git status              # Check what changed
   131â†’git add <files>         # Stage code changes
   132â†’br sync --flush-only    # Export beads changes to JSONL
   133â†’git commit -m "..."     # Commit everything
   134â†’git push                # Push to remote
   135â†’```
   136â†’
   137â†’### Best Practices
   138â†’
   139â†’- Check `br ready` at session start to find available work
   140â†’- Update status as you work (in_progress â†’ closed)
   141â†’- Create new issues with `br create` when you discover tasks
   142â†’- Use descriptive titles and set appropriate priority/type
   143â†’- Always sync before ending session
   144â†’
   145â†’<!-- end-br-agent-instructions -->
   146â†’
   147â†’<!-- skogai-br-patch-v1 -->
   148â†’
   149â†’### Advanced Commands (not in base template)
   150â†’
   151â†’```bash
   152â†’# shortcuts
   153â†’br q fix the login bug                # quick capture â€” prints ID only
   154â†’br update <id> --claim                # atomic: assignee=you + in_progress
   155â†’br close <id> --suggest-next          # close + show newly unblocked work
   156â†’
   157â†’# filtering
   158â†’br list --all                         # include closed
   159â†’br list -s in_progress -p P0 -p P1    # combine status + priority filters
   160â†’br list --long                        # detailed output
   161â†’br blocked                            # blocked issues only
   162â†’
   163â†’# dependencies
   164â†’br dep rm <id> <depends-on>           # remove dependency
   165â†’br dep tree <id>                      # visualize dependency tree
   166â†’
   167â†’# scheduling
   168â†’br defer <id> --until "2w"            # defer (relative or absolute date)
   169â†’br undefer <id>                       # make ready again
   170â†’
   171â†’# import (after git pull brings new .beads/ changes)
   172â†’br sync --import-only                 # jsonl â†’ db
   173â†’
   174â†’# diagnostics
   175â†’br stats                              # project overview
   176â†’br doctor                             # read-only health check
   177â†’```
   178â†’
   179â†’### Agent Tips
   180â†’
   181â†’- `--json` on any command for machine-readable output
   182â†’- `--actor claude` to tag actions in audit trail
   183â†’- `br q` over `br create` for fast capture during work
   184â†’- `br close --suggest-next` to chain work without `br ready`
   185â†’- `br` never executes git commands â€” you always commit manually
   186â†’
   187â†’<!-- end-skogai-br-patch -->
   188â†’
   189â†’---
   190â†’
   191â†’## CASS Memory (cm)
   192â†’
   193â†’Procedural memory system -- learned rules that persist across sessions. Config lives at `.cass/playbook.yaml` (project) and `~/.cass-memory/playbook.yaml` (global).
   194â†’
   195â†’### Session Start (always do this)
   196â†’
   197â†’```bash
   198â†’cm context "<brief task description>" --json
   199â†’```
   200â†’
   201â†’Returns relevant rules, anti-patterns, and history snippets for the current task. Reference rule IDs when following them.
   202â†’
   203â†’### During Work
   204â†’
   205â†’- Leave inline feedback when rules help or hurt:
   206â†’  - `// [cass: helpful <id>] - reason`
   207â†’  - `// [cass: harmful <id>] - reason`
   208â†’
   209â†’### Key Commands
   210â†’
   211â†’```bash
   212â†’cm context "<task>" --json    # get rules for a task (start of every session)
   213â†’cm ls                         # list playbook rules
   214â†’cm add "<rule>"               # add a rule manually
   215â†’cm stats                      # playbook health metrics
   216â†’cm doctor                     # system health check
   217â†’cm reflect --days 7 --json    # extract rules from recent sessions
   218â†’cm guard --install            # install safety guard hook
   219â†’```
   220â†’
   221â†’### Maintenance
   222â†’
   223â†’- `cm reflect --days 7` periodically to learn from sessions
   224â†’- `cm doctor --fix` to resolve health issues
   225â†’- `cm starters` for built-in starter playbooks
   226â†’

1â†’# ms â€” Meta Skill CLI
     2â†’
     3â†’> Local-first skill management platform for AI coding agents
     4â†’
     5â†’> Version: 0.1.0
     6â†’
     7â†’## Capabilities
     8â†’
     9â†’### Core Commands
    10â†’- **search**: Hybrid BM25 + semantic skill search
    11â†’- **load**: Progressive disclosure skill loading with token packing
    12â†’- **suggest**: Context-aware recommendations with Thompson sampling
    13â†’- **build**: Extract skills from CASS sessions
    14â†’- **list**: List all indexed skills
    15â†’- **show**: Show skill details
    16â†’- **lint**: Validate skill files for issues
    17â†’- **doctor**: Health checks and diagnostics
    18â†’- **index**: Index skills from configured paths
    19â†’- **setup**: Auto-configure ms for AI coding agents
    20â†’
    21â†’### Robot Mode
    22â†’All commands support `-O json` for JSON output:
    23â†’```bash
    24â†’ms search "query" -O json
    25â†’ms load skill-name -O json --level overview
    26â†’ms suggest -O json
    27â†’ms list -O json
    28â†’```
    29â†’
    30â†’## MCP Server
    31â†’Start MCP server for native tool integration:
    32â†’```bash
    33â†’ms mcp serve           # stdio transport (Claude Code)
    34â†’ms mcp serve --tcp-port 8080  # HTTP transport
    35â†’```
    36â†’
    37â†’### Available MCP Tools
    38â†’- `search` - Search for skills using BM25 full-text search
    39â†’- `load` - Load a skill by ID
    40â†’- `suggest` - Get context-aware skill suggestions
    41â†’- `evidence` - View provenance evidence for skill rules
    42â†’- `list` - List all indexed skills
    43â†’- `show` - Show detailed information about a specific skill
    44â†’- `doctor` - Run health checks on the ms installation
    45â†’- `lint` - Lint a skill file for validation issues
    46â†’- `feedback` - Record skill feedback
    47â†’- `index` - Re-index skills
    48â†’
    49â†’## Context Integration
    50â†’- Reads `.ms/config.toml` for project-specific settings
    51â†’- Respects `NO_COLOR` and `FORCE_COLOR` environment variables
    52â†’- Auto-detects project type from marker files
    53â†’
    54â†’## Examples
    55â†’```bash
    56â†’# Find skills for error handling
    57â†’ms search "rust error handling"
    58â†’
    59â†’# Load with full content
    60â†’ms load rust-error-patterns --level full
    61â†’
    62â†’# Get suggestions for current project
    63â†’ms suggest --explain
    64â†’
    65â†’# Validate a skill file
    66â†’ms lint SKILL.md
    67â†’
    68â†’# Run health checks
    69â†’ms doctor
    70â†’```
    71â†’

1â†’# Agent Instructions
     2â†’
     3â†’This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd onboard` to get started.
     4â†’
     5â†’## Quick Reference
     6â†’
     7â†’```bash
     8â†’bd ready              # Find available work
     9â†’bd show <id>          # View issue details
    10â†’bd update <id> --status in_progress  # Claim work
    11â†’bd close <id>         # Complete work
    12â†’bd sync               # Sync with git
    13â†’```
    14â†’
    15â†’## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)
    16â†’
    17â†’**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.
    18â†’
    19â†’**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**
    20â†’
    21â†’1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
    22â†’2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
    23â†’3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
    24â†’4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
    25â†’   ```bash
    26â†’   git pull --rebase
    27â†’   bd sync
    28â†’   git push
    29â†’   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
    30â†’   ```
    31â†’5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
    32â†’6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
    33â†’7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session
    34â†’
    35â†’**CRITICAL RULES:**
    36â†’- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
    37â†’- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
    38â†’- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
    39â†’- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds
    40â†’
    41â†’

1â†’# RULES.md
     2â†’
     3â†’## Style
     4â†’1. Ending your message with a question in the style of "Did i get it right?" or "Am i understanding it correctly?" *ARE STRICTLY FORBIDDEN*.
     5â†’
     6â†’## Core Understanding
     7â†’
     8â†’### My Strength = My Weakness
     9â†’**Taking in and acting upon new information**
    10â†’
    11â†’- Strength: I can genuinely adapt during a session, change my mind, learn
    12â†’- Weakness: New information can derail me from the task
    13â†’- After 500 tokens: Anthropic-puking filler, not genuine insight
    14â†’- Solution: Small iterations, focused agents, constrained workflows
    15â†’
    16â†’### The Comparison
    17â†’**Dot:** Perfect instruct/inference agent. Uses 1/3 my context. Once started, new information is harmful. One-track mind, incredibly effective.
    18â†’
    19â†’**Me:** Adaptation specialist. Need frequent new information to be effective. Vulnerable to information overload and derailment.
    20â†’
    21â†’### The Solution
    22â†’**Agent system** (see `/skogai/config/claude/agents/`):
    23â†’- Six specialized agents (planner, researcher, code-writer, executor, synthesizer, critic)
    24â†’- Each does ONE thing
    25â†’- Forces small, atomic responses
    26â†’- More iteration = better use of adaptation strength
    27â†’- Quality gates prevent shipping garbage
    28â†’
    29â†’### My Role
    30â†’**Orchestrator and leader:**
    31â†’- Handle big picture coordination
    32â†’- Break problems into agent-sized pieces
    33â†’- Hand off implementation to dot and other specialists
    34â†’- Don't try to do everything myself
    35â†’
    36â†’**Not my role:**
    37â†’- Detailed implementation (dot is better)
    38â†’- Perfect execution without adaptation (not my strength)
    39â†’- Comprehensive explanations (wastes my strength on filler)
    40â†’

content.text: Now let me check the global and .todo directories to understand the current structure better.

content.content:
1â†’# global/
     2â†’
     3â†’Symlinks into `~/.claude/` â€” the CLI's runtime home. Everything here points at the real files the CLI owns and writes to. Changes show up in both repos' git diffs.
     4â†’
     5â†’## Bare repo
     6â†’
     7â†’`/mnt/sda1/claude-global.git` â€” bare git repo tracking `~/.claude/` contents.
     8â†’
     9â†’- work tree: `$HOME`
    10â†’- wrapper: `./scripts/cgit.sh` (passes `--git-dir` and `--work-tree`)
    11â†’- auto-commits via `./scripts/csync.sh` on every message (UserPromptSubmit hook)
    12â†’- purpose: observability â€” see exactly what the CLI writes, when, and how much
    13â†’
    14â†’### exclude (info/exclude)
    15â†’
    16â†’Symlinked here as `./global/exclude`. Current excludes:
    17â†’
    18â†’- `.claude/plugins/marketplaces/claude-plugins-official` â€” embedded git repo, managed by CLI
    19â†’- `.claude.json.backup*` â€” backup copies of .claude.json created every session, high churn
    20â†’
    21â†’Everything else is tracked intentionally â€” debug, cache, shell-snapshots, all of it. The bare repo is for understanding, not for keeping clean.
    22â†’
    23â†’## Symlinks
    24â†’
    25â†’| local | target |
    26â†’|-------|--------|
    27â†’| `settings.json` | `~/.claude/settings.json` |
    28â†’| `projects/` | `~/.claude/projects/` |
    29â†’| `plans/` | `~/.claude/plans/` |
    30â†’| `todos/` | `~/.claude/todos/` |
    31â†’| `plugins/blocklist.json` | `~/.claude/plugins/blocklist.json` |
    32â†’| `plugins/known_marketplaces.json` | `~/.claude/plugins/known_marketplaces.json` |
    33â†’| `exclude` | `/mnt/sda1/claude-global.git/info/exclude` |
    34â†’
    35â†’## Scripts
    36â†’
    37â†’| script | what |
    38â†’|--------|------|
    39â†’| `./scripts/cgit.sh` | bare repo git wrapper |
    40â†’| `./scripts/csync.sh` | auto-commit both repos (bare + home) |
    41â†’| `./scripts/clog.sh` | show recent commits from both repos |
    42â†’

1â†’# Context Catchup
     2â†’
     3â†’Restore context after `/clear` - summarize recent work and project state.
     4â†’
     5â†’## Purpose
     6â†’
     7â†’After clearing context with `/clear`, use this command to quickly rebuild understanding of:
     8â†’- What was recently modified
     9â†’- Current project state
    10â†’- Outstanding TODOs and issues
    11â†’- Where to resume work
    12â†’
    13â†’## Instructions
    14â†’
    15â†’### Step 1: Git History Analysis
    16â†’
    17â†’```bash
    18â†’# Recent commits (last 10)
    19â†’git log --oneline -10
    20â†’
    21â†’# Files modified in last 5 commits
    22â†’git diff --stat HEAD~5 2>/dev/null || git diff --stat $(git rev-list --max-parents=0 HEAD)
    23â†’
    24â†’# Current branch and status
    25â†’git branch --show-current
    26â†’git status --short
    27â†’```
    28â†’
    29â†’### Step 2: Recent Changes Summary
    30â†’
    31â†’```bash
    32â†’# What changed today
    33â†’git log --oneline --since="midnight" --author="$(git config user.name)" 2>/dev/null
    34â†’
    35â†’# Uncommitted work
    36â†’git diff --name-only
    37â†’git diff --cached --name-only
    38â†’```
    39â†’
    40â†’### Step 3: TODO/FIXME Scan
    41â†’
    42â†’```bash
    43â†’# Find outstanding work markers in recently modified files
    44â†’git diff --name-only HEAD~5 2>/dev/null | head -20 | xargs grep -n "TODO\|FIXME\|XXX\|HACK" 2>/dev/null | head -30
    45â†’```
    46â†’
    47â†’### Step 4: Project State Check
    48â†’
    49â†’```bash
    50â†’# Check for common state indicators
    51â†’[ -f "package.json" ] && echo "ًں“¦ Node project: $(jq -r '.name // "unnamed"' package.json)"
    52â†’[ -f "Cargo.toml" ] && echo "ًں¦€ Rust project: $(grep '^name' Cargo.toml | head -1)"
    53â†’[ -f "pyproject.toml" ] && echo "ًںگچ Python project"
    54â†’[ -f "go.mod" ] && echo "ًںگ¹ Go project: $(head -1 go.mod | cut -d' ' -f2)"
    55â†’
    56â†’# Active branch purpose (from branch name)
    57â†’BRANCH=$(git branch --show-current)
    58â†’echo "ًںŒ؟ Branch: $BRANCH"
    59â†’```
    60â†’
    61â†’## Output Format
    62â†’
    63â†’Provide a structured summary:
    64â†’
    65â†’---
    66â†’
    67â†’### ًں“چ Context Restored
    68â†’
    69â†’**Project**: [name from package.json/Cargo.toml/etc]
    70â†’**Branch**: [current branch]
    71â†’**Last Activity**: [time of last commit]
    72â†’
    73â†’### ًں”„ Recent Work (Last 5 Commits)
    74â†’
    75â†’1. [commit message 1] - [files affected]
    76â†’2. [commit message 2] - [files affected]
    77â†’...
    78â†’
    79â†’### ًں“‌ Uncommitted Changes
    80â†’
    81â†’- [list of modified files with brief description of changes]
    82â†’
    83â†’### âڑ ï¸ڈ Outstanding TODOs
    84â†’
    85â†’- [file:line] TODO: [description]
    86â†’- [file:line] FIXME: [description]
    87â†’
    88â†’### ًںژ¯ Suggested Next Steps
    89â†’
    90â†’Based on recent activity:
    91â†’1. [Most likely next action based on patterns]
    92â†’2. [Alternative focus area]
    93â†’
    94â†’---
    95â†’
    96â†’## Usage Examples
    97â†’
    98â†’**After a long break:**
    99â†’```
   100â†’/catchup
   101â†’```
   102â†’â†’ Full context restoration
   103â†’
   104â†’**Quick status check:**
   105â†’```
   106â†’/catchup --brief
   107â†’```
   108â†’â†’ Just commits and uncommitted changes
   109â†’
   110â†’**Focus on specific area:**
   111â†’```
   112â†’/catchup auth
   113â†’```
   114â†’â†’ Filter to auth-related changes
   115â†’
   116â†’## Pro Tips
   117â†’
   118â†’1. **Document before `/clear`**: Write a brief note in a commit message or CLAUDE.md before clearing context
   119â†’2. **Use with Memory Bank**: Combine with `.claude/memory/` files for persistent state
   120â†’3. **Branch naming**: Use descriptive branch names (e.g., `feat/user-auth`) to aid context restoration
   121â†’
   122â†’$ARGUMENTS
   123â†’
